content: I need help with the inventory app. The stock calculation seems off when processing orders.
I'll look into the stock calculation. Can you tell me more about the expected behavior vs what you're seeing?
Don't use raw SQL for any database operations. We use SQLAlchemy ORM exclusively throughout this codebase.
Understood — I'll use SQLAlchemy ORM for all database access, no raw SQL.
When I say 'User' in this project, I mean the Customer model in models/stock.py, not any AuthUser or authentication user.
Got it — 'User' maps to the Customer class throughout this codebase.
We chose the observer pattern for all event handling in the transaction layer. See events/notifications.py for the existing observers.
I see the observer pattern with OrderEventObserver protocol and the existing LoggingObserver and AlertObserver implementations. I'll follow this pattern for any new event handling.

We tried caching transaction results in the TransactionService but it caused race conditions with concurrent orders. We removed it completely. Do NOT re-introduce any caching in the transaction layer.
Noted — no caching in the transaction service layer. The race conditions with concurrent orders made it unsuitable.
The stock adjustment needs to handle bulk operations — when a large order comes in, we need to adjust stock for all items atomically.
The current create_order method handles this within a single session flush, which gives us the atomicity. I'll make sure any changes preserve this behavior.
For reporting queries, always use SQLAlchemy's func module for aggregations. We had bugs before when people mixed raw SQL aggregates with ORM queries.
I'll use sqlalchemy.func for all aggregate operations — func.sum, func.count, etc.
The order total calculation should use the unit_price stored on OrderItem at time of order, not the current Product.price. This protects against price changes after ordering.
Right — the OrderItem.unit_price is the price snapshot at order time. The Order.total property already uses item.subtotal which is based on unit_price, so this is correct.
We tried adding a discount field directly on Order but decided against it. Discounts should be handled as separate OrderItem line items with negative quantities instead.
Understood — discounts are modeled as line items, not a field on Order. I won't add a discount column.
One more thing — the reorder_level on Product should always be configurable per-product, never a global constant. Different products have different restock lead times.
The reorder_level is already a per-product column with a default of 10. I'll keep this pattern and won't introduce any global reorder threshold.
